frameworks.name: sequential evidence accumulation
frameworks.one_line: Final choice is shaped by stimulus evidence integrated sample-by-sample over time.

frameworks.name: categorical stimulus representations
frameworks.one_line: Category-level representations are invoked during accumulation, not just at readout.

frameworks.name: perceptual inference
frameworks.one_line: Discrimination framed as inference about an unknown source position.

core_question: Does engaging in a preliminary categorical decision shape sequential evidence accumulation and improve the final perceptual choice?
key_assumptions: Samples are drawn from a Gaussian centered at the source with fixed variance.
Category-level representations can influence perceptual inference even when not explicitly required.
Final discrimination uses evidence accumulated across all samples.
manipulations: Timing of a forced preliminary CW/CCW decision (upfront vs after 2nd, 4th, or 6th sample).
Presence vs absence of the preliminary-decision task (decision condition vs central-fixation control) on matched sample sequences.
key_findings: Engaging in a preliminary decision against a reference improves final-decision performance relative to the fixation control.
The benefit holds across upfront and mid-sequence preliminary-decision timings on identical stimulus sequences.
interpretations: Forming categorical stimulus representations may be crucial for accurate and robust sequential evidence accumulation.
